did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Genetic Programming (GP), an evolutionary computation technique, to automatically evolve a classifier for skin cancer detection in dermoscopic images. It combines domain-specific features provided by dermatologists with Local Binary Pattern (LBP) features extracted from the images to classify melanoma versus non-melanoma lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work using Genetic Programming to evolve classifiers for skin cancer detection. It details specific methods (LBP features, domain features), a dataset (200 dermoscopic images), experimental procedures (30 runs, 5 splits), and reports new quantitative results (accuracy metrics) comparing GP against other classifiers like k-NN, SVM, and RF. The structure includes Introduction, Background, Methodology, Experiments, Results, and Conclusion, which is characteristic of a primary research article.

title: Genetic Programming for Skin Cancer Detection in Dermoscopic Images
brief_summary: This study utilizes Genetic Programming (GP) to automatically evolve classifiers for detecting melanoma in dermoscopic images, addressing the critical need for early diagnosis. The researchers combined domain-specific features provided by dermatologists with Local Binary Pattern (LBP) features extracted from the PH2 dataset, which contains 200 dermoscopic images. To handle class imbalance between melanoma and non-melanoma lesions, the GP fitness function was designed to give equal importance to both classes. The experimental evaluation compared the GP approach against several standard classifiers, including k-NN, SVM, Random Forest, and Multilayer Perceptron, using a five-split cross-validation strategy. Results demonstrated that GP significantly outperformed or achieved comparable performance to existing methods, with a maximum accuracy of 97.92% using all features and even achieving 100% accuracy in some runs with GP-selected features. The findings suggest that GP is an effective tool for skin cancer detection, particularly when leveraging both computer vision and dermatological domain knowledge.

lesion_types_studied.details: 40 instances in the PH2 dataset; classified as the positive class in the binary melanoma vs. non-melanoma task.
80 instances in the PH2 dataset; grouped with atypical nevi as 'non-melanoma'.
80 instances in the PH2 dataset; grouped with common nevi as 'non-melanoma'.
Combined class consisting of 80 common nevi and 80 atypical nevi (160 total instances) used as the negative class in the binary classification task.

all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is to 'evolve a classifier for skin cancer detection' and performs 'binary classification' to distinguish between melanoma (diseased) and non-melanoma (non-diseased) lesions. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories using features extracted from dermoscopic images.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset acquired from Pedro Hispano Hospital, Portugal. It contains dermoscopic images of skin lesions along with clinical diagnosis, binary masks, and domain-specific features provided by dermatologists based on the 7-point checklist. Images were captured using a Tuebinger Mole Analyzer system with 20x magnification and 768x560 pixel resolution.

use.training: Used for training the Genetic Programming classifiers. The dataset was randomly split into training and test sets five times (5-fold split), with 70% of instances (140 images) used for training in each split.
use.testing: Used for testing the evolved classifiers. In each of the five splits, 30% of instances (60 images) were used for testing.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Fitness function (based on True Positive and True Negative rates)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the performance of the Genetic Programming (GP) classifier against other non-GP classifiers (k-NN, NB, SVM, J48, RF, MLP) and determine if the differences in accuracy were statistically significant.
Used as the threshold for the Wilcoxon signed-rank test to determine statistical significance.
To summarize the accuracy results across 30 independent runs for GP and non-GP methods in the second experiment, and across 5 splits in the first experiment.
To evaluate the performance of the classifiers by splitting the dataset into training and testing sets five times, ensuring the results are robust and not dependent on a single split.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no statistically significant difference in performance between the Genetic Programming (GP) classifier and the compared non-GP classifiers (k-NN, NB, SVM, J48, RF, MLP).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a statistically significant difference in performance between the GP classifier and the compared non-GP classifiers.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The Wilcoxon signed-rank test was used with a significance level of 5% to determine if GP could compete with other classifiers. Symbols '+' and '-' indicated significantly better or worse performance respectively, while '=' indicated no statistically significant difference.

preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of dermoscopic images to gray-scale before applying Local Binary Pattern (LBP) feature extraction
Use of binary masks for segmentation (provided in the PH2 dataset)

Extraction of domain-specific features based on the 7-point checklist by dermatologists (Asymmetry, Pigment network, Dots/Globules, Streaks, Regression areas, Blue-whitish veil, and presence/absence of Colors: white, red, light-brown, dark-brown, blue-gray, black)
conclusions: Genetic Programming (GP) significantly outperformed or achieved comparable performance to existing methods (k-NN, NB, SVM, J48, RF, MLP) for skin cancer detection in dermoscopic images.

Combining domain-specific features provided by dermatologists with Local Binary Pattern (LBP) features extracted from images led to better classification performance than using either feature set alone.

GP demonstrated effectiveness as a feature selection method, achieving 100% accuracy in some runs when using GP-selected features, and showing good performance compared to classifiers using all features.
The study validated that integrating knowledge from both dermatology and computer vision domains improves the identification of diseased and non-diseased instances.

The standard overall classification accuracy is not appropriate for this imbalanced dataset; instead, a fitness function giving equal importance to both classes (based on true positive and true negative rates) is required.

Computer analysis should be developed to assist, not replace, physicians in the diagnosis of skin cancer lesions, as the best diagnostic results are achieved by combining trained computer classifiers with experienced dermatologist diagnosis.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single, small public dataset (PH2) with only 200 images, limiting generalizability and external validation.

The dataset is highly imbalanced (4:1 ratio of non-melanoma to melanoma), necessitating a custom fitness function (average of sensitivity and specificity) rather than standard accuracy to avoid bias toward the majority class.

The paper explicitly states that standard classification accuracy is inappropriate for this imbalanced dataset, as it can be misleadingly high by correctly classifying only the non-diseased instances.

The authors emphasize that computer-aided diagnosis should assist, not replace, physicians, noting that the best diagnostic results come from combining trained classifiers with experienced dermatologist diagnosis.

GP demonstrated strong performance as a feature selection method, achieving 100% accuracy in some runs by selecting a subset of features, and SVM using GP-selected features achieved 0.0 standard deviation.
The study is the first to utilize Genetic Programming for skin cancer detection in dermoscopic image data, comparing it against k-NN, NB, SVM, J48, RF, and MLP.

Statistical significance was assessed using the Wilcoxon signed-rank test at a 5% significance level, with results reported as mean ± standard deviation over 30 independent runs for GP and 5 splits for others.
The domain-specific features were provided by dermatologists based on the 7-point checklist, highlighting the importance of integrating clinical knowledge with computer vision techniques.
